elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate chemistry in the compression layer behind the interstellar shock waves, where molecular cloud formation starts.
elements.sent-2411-002: We perform three-dimensional magnetohydrodynamics simulations of converging flows of atomic gas with shock parameters of inclination between the interstellar magnetic field and the shock wave, pre-shock density, and shock velocity.
elements.sent-2411-003: Then we derive 1D mean-flow models, along which we calculate a detailed gas-grain chemical reaction network as a post process with various chemical parameters, i.e. cosmic-ray ionization rate, abundances of PAHs, and metals in the gas phase.
elements.sent-2411-004: While carbon chains reach their peak abundances when atomic carbon is dominant in the pseudo-time-dependent models of molecular clouds, such behavior is less significant in our models since the visual extinction of the compression layer is low ($\la 1$ mag) when atomic carbon is abundant.
elements.sent-2411-005: Carbon chains, CN, and HCN increase at $A_V \ga 1$ mag, where the gas-phase C/O ratio increases due to water ice formation.
elements.sent-2411-006: Shock parameters affect the physical structure and the evolutional timescale of the compression layer, and thus molecular evolution.
elements.sent-2411-007: Carbon chains are more abundant in models with higher post-shock density and slower gas accumulation.
elements.sent-2411-008: We calculate molecular column densities in the compression layer and compare them with the observations of diffuse and translucent clouds, which show reasonable agreement for water ice, carbon chains, and HCO$^+$.
elements.sent-2411-009: The observed variation of their column densities could be due to the difference in shock parameters and chemical parameters.
elements.sent-2411-010: The column density of CN is overestimated, for which we discuss possible reasons.
elements.sent-2411-011: Molecular cloud formation is a pivotal astrophysical process that sets the initial conditions of star formation.
elements.sent-2411-012: Observational and theoretical work in recent years shows that the molecular cloud formation is triggered by interstellar shock waves \citep{2015A A...580A..49I,2023ASPC..534..233P , which are driven by e.g. supernovae \citep{1974ApJ...188..501C,1977ApJ...218..148M,1988ApJ...334..252C and HII regions \citep{1978ppim.book.....S .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Expanding hot gas sweeps up and compresses the HI gas in galactic disks, making dense cold gas behind the shock wave.
elements.sent-2411-014: Magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) simulations show that shock waves can also induce filamentary structures characteristic of molecular clouds \citep{2013ApJ...774L..31I,2018PASJ...70S..53I,2021ApJ...916...83A .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Observations also indicate the relationship between filament formation and shock waves \citep[e.g.][]{2018PASJ...70...96A .}
elements.sent-2411-016: The cloud formation process is important not only for astrophysics but also for astrochemistry.
elements.sent-2411-017: To date, more than 200 interstellar molecules have been detected \citep{McGuire_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Although there are many studies on the chemical modeling of interstellar clouds, most of them calculate the chemical abundance of molecular clouds as a function of time assuming a static and uniform physical condition and that H is already converted to H$_2$, i.e. pseudo-time-dependent model \citep{1980ApJ...239..151P,2019ApJ...871..238H .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Some pioneering work studied the chemical evolution of a molecular cloud in its formation stage from HI gas with time-dependent physical parameters \citep{2004ApJ...612..921B,2010A A...515A..66H,2015A A...584A.124F .}
elements.sent-2411-020: They used one-dimensional (1D) steady-state shock models and solved the chemical evolution in the post-shock gas as a function of time, which is converted to visual extinction.
elements.sent-2411-021: Their models succeeded in providing the basic chemical properties regarding main gaseous species such as H$_2$ and CO \citep{2004ApJ...612..921B , ice species on grain surface \citep{2010A A...515A..66H , and water deuteration \citep{2015A A...584A.124F .}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Since H$_2$ does not emit lines in cold ($\la$ 100 K) regions, theoretical studies on major molecular abundances such as atomic C and CO relative to H$_2$ are essential for observational studies of cloud formation.
elements.sent-2411-023: Chemistry in interstellar clouds is mostly non-equilibrium, and thus the astrochemistry of various molecules, ices, and isotope ratios could also be used to validate the cloud formation hypothesis via comparison with observations.
elements.sent-2411-024: While these previous studies adopted a 1D shock model, MHD simulations in recent years show that multi-dimensional structures with magnetic fields are essential in molecular cloud formation.
elements.sent-2411-025: Without the magnetic field, molecular clouds can be directly formed by a single shock compression, even from pure warm neutral medium (WNM) \citep{2002ApJ...564L..97K,2006ApJ...648.1052H,Vazquez-Semadeni_2006,2007A A...465..431H .}
elements.sent-2411-026: On the other hand, with the interstellar magnetic fields with typical strength of several $\mathrm{\mu G}$, the magnetic pressure can prevent atomic gas from evolving into molecular clouds and the gas is converted to cold neutral medium (CNM) \citep{2008ApJ...687..303I,Inoue_2009,2009ApJ...695..248H,2015MNRAS.451.3340K,2019ApJ...873....6I,Iwasaki_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: The inclination of the interstellar magnetic field against the shock wave directly affects the evolution of compression layers or the initial phase of molecular cloud formation.
elements.sent-2411-028: In the present work, we investigate the chemical evolution of atomic gas to molecular gas based on the 3D MHD simulation of interstellar shock compression.
elements.sent-2411-029: We aim to adopt detailed chemical models that include thousands of chemical reactions to investigate the dependence of various molecular abundances in the gas phase and ice mantles on the shock parameters.
elements.sent-2411-030: However, it is computationally too expensive to directly couple such a detailed chemical network with the multi-dimensional MHD simulations.
elements.sent-2411-031: We thus construct 1D models of the physical evolution of interstellar medium (ISM) from the results of the 3D MHD simulations and conduct the network calculation as a post process.
elements.sent-2411-032: Specifically, we model the mean evolution of the compression layers based on the 3D MHD simulations and conduct the chemical network calculations along the flow.
elements.sent-2411-033: This is an extension of the previous chemical models based on 1D shock and is the first step in studying the detailed chemical evolution of the compression layers taking into account the effect of multi-dimensional flow.
elements.sent-2411-034: In both the original 3D simulations and the derived 1D models, the column density and thus the visual extinction $A_V$ of the compression layer increases with time.
elements.sent-2411-035: In the present work, we focus on the early phase of molecular cloud formation and conduct a series of calculations until $A_V$ reaches $\sim 5$ mag.
elements.sent-2411-036: Typical hydrogen number density in the compression layer is $10^2-10^3\,\mathrm{cm^{-3}$.}
elements.sent-2411-037: We thus compare our results with observations of diffuse and translucent clouds \citep{2006ARA A..44..367S .}
elements.sent-2411-038: The chemical characteristics of diffuse clouds have been studied by absorption lines of molecules observed toward various kinds of light sources such as stars, massive star-forming regions, and extragalactic sources \citep[e.g.][]{1996A A...307..237L,2002A A...384.1054L,2006A A...448..253L,2008ApJ...687.1075S,2010A A...518L.110G,2010A A...520A..20G,2014A A...564A..64L,2022ApJ...928...79R,2023A A...670A.111K .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Such observations reveal statistics of the chemical properties of diffuse clouds in our galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-040: For example, while the column densities of different kinds of interstellar molecules are well correlated, they also show a scatter of orders of magnitude \citep[e.g. see Figure 4.. to 6.
elements.sent-2411-041: The origin of this scatter is still not well understood.
elements.sent-2411-042: We compare our results with these observations to investigate if the scatter can be explained by the chemistry with different shock models and/or other parameters such as cosmic-ray ionization rate.
elements.sent-2411-043: Even in such an early phase of cloud evolution, it is important to consider not only gas-phase reactions but also grain-surface reactions.
elements.sent-2411-044: For example, \citet{2015A A...584A.124F showed that water ice becomes one of the major oxygen reservoirs at $A_V\sim 1$ mag.}
elements.sent-2411-045: It is well known that water ice formation enhances the elemental C/O ratio in the gas phase, which significantly affects carbon chemistry \citep[e.g.][]{Pratap_1997,Terzieva_1998, Aikawa_2003, Ruaud_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-046: If the C/O ratio is $< 1$ and CO formation is efficient, almost all carbon is used to form CO and the formation of other C-bearing molecules is suppressed.
elements.sent-2411-047: On the other hand, in the case of C/O > 1, even if all oxygen is used for the CO formation, there is leftover carbon, which can be used to form carbon chains and CN-bearing molecules.
elements.sent-2411-048: The water formation starts from the adsorption of O atoms on to grain surfaces, where they are hydrogenated.
elements.sent-2411-049: Since the adsorption timescale is comparable to the cloud formation timescale $\sim 60\, \mathrm{Myr (A_V/1\,\mathrm{mag )(v_\mathrm{flow /10\,\mathrm{km/s )^{-1 (n/1\,\mathrm{cm^{-3 )^{-1}$ \citep{Inoue_2012 , where $v_\mathrm{flow}$ and $n$ are the inflowing gas velocity and density, respectively, the C/O ratio in the gas phase also depends on the physical evolution of molecular clouds.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: We look into these properties using detailed chemical network calculations.
elements.sent-2411-051: The rest of the paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-052: The details of the shock model are summarized in Section \ref{sec:phys .}
elements.sent-2411-053: The method of chemical network calculations is described in Section \ref{sec:chem .}
elements.sent-2411-054: The results of the network calculation including a parameter survey are shown in Section \ref{sec:result .}
elements.sent-2411-055: The comparison between our results and observations of diffuse clouds and other discussions are presented in Section \ref{sec:discussion .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Finally, we summarize our results in Section \ref{sec:conclusion .}
elements.sent-2411-057: In this section, we describe the setup of our MHD simulations and the methodology for modeling the mean evolution of the compression layers along the shock propagation, which are used for chemical network calculations.

elements.sent-2411-068: We simulate the interstellar shock of a conversing flow (Figure \ref{fig:mhd_setting ).}
elements.sent-2411-069: The size of our simulation box is $L_x=8\,\mathrm{pc}$, and $L_y=L_z=4\,\mathrm{pc}$.
elements.sent-2411-070: This calculation region is divided into $512 \times 256 \times 256$ cells with the spatial resolution of $\Delta x \simeq 0.02\,\mathrm{pc}$, so that the typical cooling length of WNM ($\sim$ a few $\mathrm{pc}$) is well-resolved \citep{2020ApJ...905...95K .}
elements.sent-2411-071: Initially, the gas is completely atomic.
elements.sent-2411-072: The velocity field is directed to the $x=0$ plane in order to initiate shock compression at the center of our calculation box; \begin{equation}u_x=-V_0\tanh{\left(\frac{x}{0.5\,\mathrm{pc}}\right)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-073: The initial magnetic field is on the $x-y$ plane; it is uniform and inclined from the $x$-axis with an angle $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-074: The initial field strength is set to be $2\,\mathrm{\mu G}$.
elements.sent-2411-075: The averaged hydrogen number density of the initial atomic gas is $\bar{n _\mathrm{H,0}$.}
elements.sent-2411-076: We set the initial density dispersion by $P(\log n)\propto \vb*{k ^{-2}$ as the seed of turbulence, where $P(\log n)$ is the power spectrum and $\vb*{k}$ is the wave number and varies from $2\pi/L_y$ to $32\pi/L_y$.}
elements.sent-2411-077: The mean density dispersion is set to be $1\%$.
elements.sent-2411-078: In reality, the density dispersion would be larger and the initial gas would be two-phase (i.e., WNM and CNM).
elements.sent-2411-079: We set the small density dispersion for the fiducial model since the main motivation of the present work is to study the chemical evolution of the mean flow.
elements.sent-2411-080: More realistic simulations with a higher dispersion are postponed to future studies.
elements.sent-2411-081: The initial gas pressure is set to be uniform with a value given by the thermal equilibrium state at the averaged density $\bar{n _\mathrm{H,0}$ and $A_V=0\,\mathrm{mag}$.}
elements.sent-2411-082: The boundary conditions of the $x$ direction are set so that the initial gas is continuously injected with velocity $V_0$, but the pressure of the injected gas is set to be the equilibrium value at $A_V=A_V^\mathrm{eff}$, which is given below.
elements.sent-2411-083: We adopt periodic conditions for $y$ and $z$ directions.
elements.sent-2411-084: In order to calculate the local net heating rate (equation (\ref{equ:heating )), it is necessary to evaluate the visual extinction (or total hydrogen column density).}
elements.sent-2411-085: In this study, we use a two-ray approximation.
elements.sent-2411-086: The effective visual extinction is calculated by integrating total hydrogen number density from the $+x$ and $-x$ boundaries: \begin{equation}\begin{split} A_V^\mathrm{eff}(x,y,z) = -\frac{1}{\alpha}\ln\, \biggl[\frac{1}{2}\exp(-\alpha A_V^+(x,y,z)) +\frac{1}{2}\exp(-\alpha A_V^-(x,y,z))\biggr], \end{split}\end{equation} \begin{equation}A_V^+(x,y,z)=\frac{1}{N_\mathrm{H,0}}\int_{-L_x/2}^{x}\,(n_\mathrm{H}(x',y,z)-\bar{n}_{\mathrm{H,0}})\,\dd x',\end{equation} \begin{equation}A_V^-(x,y,z)=\frac{1}{N_\mathrm{H,0}}\int_{x}^{L_x/2}\,(n_\mathrm{H}(x',y,z)-\bar{n}_{\mathrm{H,0}})\,\dd x',\end{equation} where $N_\mathrm{H,0 =2.0\times 10^{21}\,\mathrm{cm^{-2 }$ corresponds to $A_V=1$ mag.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: This method is justified because the shock-compressed region has a sheet-like structure.
elements.sent-2411-088: Here, we subtract the pre-shock density $\bar{n _\mathrm{H,0}$ so that $A_V^\mathrm{eff}$ does not depend on the size of the simulation box (see also \ref{sec:analysis_flow_1dmodel ).}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Our calculations are conducted with Athena++ \citep{2020ApJS..249....4S and simulate the evolution up to 10 Myr or until the shock fronts reach the calculation boundary of the $x$ direction.}
elements.sent-2411-090: The calculation time is $(1-5)\times 10^4$ core-hours on the Cray XC50 supercomputer, depending on initial parameters.

elements.sent-2411-092: We thus adopt the parameters, $\bar{n _\mathrm{H,0 , V_0$, and $\sin\theta$, as listed in Table \ref{tab:Table1 and choose N10V10sin020 as our fiducial model.}}}

elements.sent-2411-099: The structure of the compression region becomes a quasi-steady state in less than 1 Myr after the onset of the calculation.
elements.sent-2411-100: Then the compression layer expands with almost constant velocity due to the continuous flow.
elements.sent-2411-101: In the models of a small inclination angle ($\sin\,\theta=0.05$), the compression layer becomes highly turbulent due to the nonlinear thin-shell instability \citep{Vishniac_1994 .}
elements.sent-2411-102: The layer has a wide density distribution, and both clumpy and sparse regions appear.
elements.sent-2411-103: When the inclination is large ($\sin\,\theta=0.5$), magnetic pressure dominates, and turbulence is suppressed.
elements.sent-2411-104: With a moderate inclination angle ($\sin\,\theta=0.2$), the magnetic field suppresses the instability, but magnetic pressure is not strong enough to prevent compression.
elements.sent-2411-105: In such conditions, the compression region becomes thin and the gas density becomes high compared with the models with small or large inclinations.
elements.sent-2411-106: These features of the compression layer and their dependence on $\sin \theta$ are consistent with \citet{2019ApJ...873....6I , who showed that the most efficient compression is achieved at the critical angle $\theta_\mathrm{cr}$.}

elements.subsubsection-2411-005: Conversion of the 3D model to the 1D model
elements.sent-2411-107: In the present study, we investigate the detailed chemical evolution of a test particle (i.e. fluid parcel) advecting at the mean velocity field along the $x$-axis.
elements.sent-2411-108: Here, we describe how we obtain the temporal variation of the physical parameters of the test particle.
elements.sent-2411-109: Firstly, we calculated the one-dimensional structure of the converging flow.
elements.sent-2411-110: We focus on the left side of the simulation box.
elements.sent-2411-111: We average the hydrogen number density $n_\mathrm{H}$, gas temperature $T_\mathrm{gas}$, and $x$-component of the velocity field $v_x$ along the $y$ and $z$-direction at each position $x$ and time, \begin{equation}\bar{X}_\mathrm{M}(x,t)=\frac{\iint n_\mathrm{H}(x,y,z,t)X(x,y,z,t)\,\dd y\dd z}{\iint n_\mathrm{H}(x,y,z,t)\,\dd y\dd z},\end{equation} where $X$ represents either $n_\mathrm{H}$, or $T_\mathrm{g}$, or $v_x$.

elements.sent-2411-417: We investigated the chemical evolution during the formation of molecular clouds by an interstellar shock wave.
elements.sent-2411-418: We conducted 3D MHD simulations of the converging flow of HI gas and investigated the effects of shock parameters, i.e. initial inclination of the interstellar magnetic field, initial shock velocity, and initial density.
elements.sent-2411-419: We analyzed the averaged physical structure of the compression layer, and derived the temporal variation of physical parameters of test particles that advect following the mean velocity field.
elements.sent-2411-420: Then we conducted the chemical network calculations along the mean flows.
elements.sent-2411-421: We also investigated how the molecular evolution depends on chemical parameters, i.e. abundance of refractory elements, cosmic-ray ionization rate, and the existence of PAHs.
elements.sent-2411-422: Our main findings are summarized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-423: The averaged post-shock gas density sensitively depends on the shock parameters, especially the inclination angle of the magnetic field to the conversing flow.
elements.sent-2411-424: This trend is consistent with the previous work \citep{2019ApJ...873....6I .}
elements.sent-2411-425: In classical pseudo-time-dependent models, the abundances of carbon-chain molecules, CN, and HCN reach the peak values when the C atom is the dominant carbon carrier.
elements.sent-2411-426: This trend is much less significant in our model, since the visual extinction of the compression layer is relatively low ($A_V \la 1$ mag) when C atom is abundant.
elements.sent-2411-427: Carbon chains increase at $A_V \ga 1$ mag, where the gas-phase C/O ratio increases due to water ice formation.
elements.sent-2411-428: The chemical desorption of hydrogenated nitrogen from the grain surface increases the abundance of NH$_n$ in the gas phase at $A_V\la 1$ mag.
elements.sent-2411-429: It also helps the formation of CN and HCN by the reactions between NH$_n$ and C$^+$.
elements.sent-2411-430: Molecular evolution in the cloud formation, i.e. the molecular abundances as a function of the visual extinction of the compression layer, depends on the gas density in the compression layer and the accumulation timescale, and thus on shock parameters.
elements.sent-2411-431: Carbon chains are especially enhanced in the models with high post-shock density and slow accumulation.
elements.sent-2411-432: The abundance of refractory elements, cosmic-ray ionization rate, and the existence of PAHs mainly affect the abundance of positively charged species (e.g. C$^+$, S$^+$, HCO$^+$, and H$_3^+$) and the ionization degree.
elements.sent-2411-433: Carbon chains are more abundant in the models with higher $\zeta_\mathrm{CR}$, in which more C$^+$ is produced by CO + He$^+$.
elements.sent-2411-434: The molecular column densities along the direction of shock propagation are calculated from our models and compared with the observations of diffuse and translucent molecular clouds.
elements.sent-2411-435: The column densities of HCO$^+$ and carbon-chain molecules show reasonable agreement with observations.
elements.sent-2411-436: The variation of their column densities in the observations could be due to the difference in both shock parameters and chemical parameters.
elements.sent-2411-437: The column density of water ice is in reasonable agreement with the observations, as well.
elements.sent-2411-438: The column densities of N-bearing molecules, i.e. CN and HCN, are overestimated in our models compared with the observed values.
elements.sent-2411-439: Their abundance could be reduced if N atom is converted to N$_2$, NH$_3$ ice, or NH$_3$ salt more efficiently by e.g. multiple shocks.
elements.sent-2411-440: Enhancement of HCO$^+$ at low $A_{\rm V}$ by extra heating provided by multiple shocks could also improve the agreement between our model with the observation.
elements.sent-2411-441: While we focused on the averaged structure of the compression layers formed by a single shock wave, we will continue our study with more realistic 3D MHD simulations coupled with molecular evolution to investigate the effect of turbulence, the two-phase gas with density fluctuations, and multiple shocks.

elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=0.9\columnwidth]{figure/bistability2.pdf} \caption{The major chemical reactions for atomic ions in HMCR16PAHF. The reactions encircled by the red and blue triangles show the main reactions before and after the sudden decline of ionization degree, respectively.} \label{fig:bistability}
elements.sent-2411-447: In model HMCR16PAHF i.e. the high-metal abundance model without PAHs, the molecular abundance and the ionization degree suddenly change at $A_V\simeq 1.9$ mag.
elements.sent-2411-448: This is due to the change in reactants of major charge carriers, atomic ions such as S$^+$ and Si$^+$, the abundance of which also sharply declines at $A_V\simeq 1.9$ mag.
elements.sent-2411-449: Figure \ref{fig:bistability displays the major reactions for the atomic ions.}
elements.sent-2411-450: Before the decline, they mainly react with electrons.
elements.sent-2411-451: The typical rate coefficient for the radiative recombination is about $10^{-11}\,\mathrm{cm^3\,s^{-1 }$.}
elements.sent-2411-452: As the C/O ratio increases in the gas phase, long carbon-chain molecules ($\mathrm{C_nH}$) become abundant, as explained in Section \ref{sec:fiducial .}
elements.sent-2411-453: They react with electrons to form anions ($\mathrm{C_nH^-}$), which become the major reactant with atomic ions: \begin{equation}\label{equ:recomb_metal_ani} \mathrm{C_nH^-} + \ce{M^+} \rightarrow \mathrm{C_nH} + \ce{M},\end{equation} where M represents the atoms that contribute to the ionization degree.
elements.sent-2411-454: The typical rate coefficient for this reaction is about $10^{-7}\,\mathrm{cm^3\,s^{-1 }$ \citep{Harada_2008 , which is orders of magnitude higher than that of radiative recombination.}}
elements.sent-2411-455: The ionization degree therefore decreases when the reaction (\ref{equ:recomb_metal_ani ) becomes effective.}
elements.sent-2411-456: In addition, carbon-chain cations are mainly destroyed by dissociative recombination with an electron, which becomes less effective after the electron abundance decreases.
elements.sent-2411-457: This helps carbon-chain molecules and anions become more abundant and the ionization degree further decreases.
elements.sent-2411-458: We note that the major cation remains the same, i.e. S$^+$ and Si$^+$, before and after the decline of ionization degree.
elements.sent-2411-459: CO also shows a temporal decrease and its chemistry changes at $A_V\simeq 1.9$ mag in model HMCR16PAHF.
elements.sent-2411-460: In the fiducial model with PAHs (HMCR16PAHT), CO is mainly formed by two neutral-neutral reactions: OH + C and C$_2$ + O.
elements.sent-2411-461: Formation of OH formation is initiated by the reaction between H$_3^+$ and O atom.
elements.sent-2411-462: Without PAHs, the electron abundance is high, which decreases the abundances of H$_3^+$ and OH.
elements.sent-2411-463: Thus the reaction of C$_2$ + O dominates in CO formation in model HMCR16PAHF.
elements.sent-2411-464: As $A_V$ increases O atom starts to deplete from the gas phase, which makes the CO formation less efficient.
elements.sent-2411-465: At $A_V\simeq 1.9$ mag, carbon-chain molecules become abundant, and CO abundance also suddenly increases by reactions between the carbon-chain molecules and O atoms.
